system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Jazz Standard Study #7: On Green Dolphin Street
Pages 116-132 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

The next jazz standard in our studies is the iconic tune On Green Dol-
phin Street. This song is not only popular and essential to know, but it
has some unique characteristics that | think are worth taking a closer
look at.

We are coming back into the world of functional harmony, a departure
from So What, but there are still some things in On Green Dolphin
Street that may raise some eyebrows.

On Green Dolphin Street ie

On Green Dolphin Street was composed by Bronislaw Kaper with lyrics by Ned
Washington. It was originally composed for the 1947 film Green Dolphin Street.
The Jimmy Dorsey Orchestra recorded it for the film. It was then adopted by
jazz musicians such as Ahmad Jamal, Chet Baker and Miles Dauis. This song is
commonly performed in the keys of concert C and Eb major.

Even though our study of On Green Dolphin Street will be in Concert C
major, | would encourage you to be able to play it in concert Eb major
as well. If you perform it with other jazz musicians, it could be called in
either key. It’s best to be prepared for either of them and transposing
is an excellent exercise in and of itself.


As per usual, here are some recordings | suggest listening to.

>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Miles Davis from “Kind of Blue (The Legacy
Addition)” 1959

The George Shearing Quintet Feat. Nancy Wilson
from “The Swingin’s Mutual” 1992 (Remaster)

Ahmad Jamal from “The Complete Ahmad Jamal
Trio Argo Sessions” 1956-62

Bill Evans from “The Complete Fantasy Recordings”
1989

Johnny Hartman from “For Trane” 1995 (Remaster)

As | always suggest, do some additional research and find some other
recordings to listen to. These are just a place to start.

Let’s dive into our study and take a look at the chord map, guide tone
chart, and chords analysis of On Green Dolphin Street.

Before we jump into looking at the harmony and how it works, | think it’s
important to note how this song is commonly performed. Commonly,
meaning, it doesn’t have to be performed this way but is the default.

Most of the time the first 8 bars are played in a Latin or straight-eighth
feel, and the 1% and 2°¢ endings are swung. You'll hear this happen
in many recordings you investigate. This is just something you should
know.

Let’s take a look at the color code for this chords analysis:
Blue = the parent major key

Green = shift to the parent minor
‘Yellow: = new key center a minor 3 up
‘Red = the relative minor

(Orange = a ii-V of iii

On Green Dolphin Street has a lot going on with the harmony, so let’s
get right to it. Take a look at the first 8 bars.

Cua’ Cun’

I i Minor

D7/C Deuas7/C Cun’ Ewin? A’

* 117 bIImaj7 I iii-VI7-----------

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK [MPx}



The form starts right on the tonic. Nothing mysterious there until we
get to bar 3 where we make a sudden switch to the i minor. We’ve seen
this happen to the IV chord before, back in our study of All of Me, but
this time it’s happening to the | chord.

When it comes to improvising over these two chords, don’t panic. The
only difference between a major 7 chord and a minor 7 chord is the mi-
nor has a b3 and a b7/. Make this adjustment and you are good to go!

| think the part that confuses most is bars 5-8. We start out with a
dominant || chord, which as we have discussed in past chapters is not
abnormal. You can often turn non-tonic minor chords into dominant
7ths.

Notice as well that this is a slash chord (D7/C). That means this chord
has a C in the bass. Often times in jazz we call this a “pedal.”

The next chord is the most puzzling: the bllmaj7.

The easiest way to think of this chord is a substitute for the V chord. In
other words, G7 is being replaced by a Domaj7. So really, bars 5-7 are
a ii-V-I with a replacement for the V chord.

Sometimes this is called a tritone substitution, meaning, a chord is
being substituted by another chord with a root a tritone interval away.
In most cases, a tritone substitution replaces one dominant 7 chord for
another dominant 7. This is clearly not happening here, as the bllmaj7
has a major quality.

Another way to think of this chord is as a simple chromatic passing
chord between the ii and | chord. Chromaticism is a common charac-
teristic in jazz, and this to me is what seems to be going on here.

A simple iii-VI7 progression functions as a turnaround to the ii chord in
bar 9.

The big question that lingers is how do we improvise over this chord
progression? Luckily, we will cover some musical examples in this
chapters Improv Lesson. But for now, let’s move on to the next section
worth taking a look into.


Fain? B’” EPwas” Dain? G’

* ji-V-I (New Key Center a Minor 3rd Up) -- --------------- ii-V (Turn Around)

In bars 13-15 we have a key change. One reason | chose On Green Dol-
phin Street as an important standard to study is this very section here.

There are other standards you will come across that have key center
changes a minor 3" up from the parent tonic. This is a common com-
positional technique in jazz standards, and you will likely see it again
the more you study them.

Of course, we are dealing with a simple ii-V-| chord progression. I’m
sure that you are seeing from our studies just how vital major and
minor ii-V-| chord progressions are. So luckily, the only curveball with
these bars is the key change, and nothing too harmonically abnormal.
Bar 16 is a ii-V chord progression that turns around back to the parent
tonic at the top of the form.

Let’s take a look at the final section.

| 2
Dun? = Dwin7/C Bn 75) 709” Amn? = Awn7/G) Fawn?) B70

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK



First, take note of the descending bass notes. In bar 17, the second
Dmin7 has a C in the bass which connects to the Bmin7(b5) by a half
step. In bar 19, the second Amin7 has a G in the bass which connects
by a half step to the F#min7(b5).

If you are a bass player, this kind of descending bass line movement
is fairly common. Regardless of what instrument you play, take some
time and play through the bass notes of bars 17-20 to see how they
flow.

Bars 18-19 move into the relative minor. We’ve seen this before. In this
case, I’ve chosen to label the chords in relation to the tonic rather than
a new key center.

I’ve highlighted bar 20 because it’s a ii-V of iii, which connects into a
long diatonic cycle of 4ths to end the song in bars 21-24.

It’s important to take a step back and look at the whole picture. We
have a sizeable diatonic sequence that is connected together with
a descending bass line movement, and supported by ii-V secondary
dominants. | think the harmony flows beautifully!


IMPROV LESSON
Using Motwic Development

For this chapter’s improv lesson, | want to focus on those first 8 bars
of Green Dolphin. I’ve already explained how the chords in the progres-
sion function together, but | want to help you start to develop some
musical ideas.

Whenever | come across a section within a piece of music that seems
confusing or causes me trouble, | abide by a simple rule: keep it simple.

Often times our instinct as improvisers in these situations is to fill up
space and play as many notes over each chord as possible. But in re-
ality, this is the last thing we want to do, both for survival and musical
reasons.

When we come across these kinds of chord progressions, we immedi-
ately want to ask the question, “What are the defining notes of each
chord?” In other words, which notes differ from the chord before it.

What's the difference between a Cmaj7 and a Cmin7? What's the
difference between a D7 and a Dbmaj7? These are important ques-
tions that need to be asked.

In the spirit of keeping it simple, I’ve composed five motifs to help you
get some ideas for navigating this chord progression.

>> WHAT IS A MOTIF?

In music, a motif is a short rhythmic or melodic passage that is repeat-
ed or evoked in various parts of a composition.

127


Recorded 8va

For each one of these motifs, | will take a basic rhythmic structure or
melodic idea and do my best repeat that idea over each chord pre-
sented in this progression. Your job is to play through each of these
and observe how these chords are being connected.

Cua? Cmin?

“ap rR, pp Pee bp pee bp pe PR
D7/Cc D>uas7/C Cun’ Emin” A’
aE ie 5 ote £95 pp pt Ff et le

First notice the rhythmic pattern: 1 quarter note, 3 eighth notes, and a
dotted quarter. Then 4 eighth notes with a tie to a half note.

Next, observe how | make as few changes to the melodic lines as pos-
sible. | try to adjust the notes minimally, only to accommodate the
changes necessary from one chord to another.

For example, look at the how the notes change between the Cmoaj7
and the Cmin7. The only changes made is a B natural to a Bb and an
E natural to an Eb. If you’re paying attention, you'll note those are the
3rds and 7ths.

Let’s move on to the next motif.



Cau” Cun’

patie fp o ites, 9 pie 6,» fle ..

For this one, | have kept the same rhythmic pattern as seen in Mo-
tif #1. The main thing | want to demonstrate is the use of chromatic
passing tones.

We’ve seen these before in other studies, but | think it’s time for a solid
definition.

>> WHAT IS A CHROMATIC PASSING TONE?

A chromatic passing tone is a non-diatonic note that connects two dia-
tonic notes in a passage, usually by half steps.

Example: C-C#-D

So, the rhythmic pattern remains the same, and the melodic line is al-
tered only slightly to accommodate a chromatic passing tone. | have
mentioned before, chromaticism is a defining characteristic of jazz
language, and it’s good to incorporate it into your improvisation.

Composing simple motifs like these and experimenting with them by
making slight adjustments is a great practice. It can teach you how to
develop an idea and what options are available to you.

Take a look at the next one.

This time I’ve changed up the rhythmic pattern and melodic line. This
is more of a bebop style line, with long flowing eighth notes and brief
eighth note rests to break it up a bit.

Once again, | want you to notice that I’ve made as few changes to the
actual notes as possible from chord to chord. In fact, if you look back
at the last two motifs, the note changes are the same (disregarding
the chromatic passing tones).

This motif helps you understand how you can play longer phrases over
this chord progression all while still outlining the chord changes per-
fectly.

On to the next motif.

In Motif #4, I’ve returned to simpler rhythmic patterns, but I’ve also
varied them a bit. They are all similar, but I’ve allowed for some wiggle
room on how they lay out in bars 5 and 6.

In addition, the note choices are more liberal. For example, instead of
ending the first bar of Cmin11 on a G like | did on the Cmaj7, | ended it
onan F because | wanted to go for that minor 11* sound.

This isn’t something | have explicitly said in this book so far, so I'll
mention it now. Most chords in jazz are labeled as 7 chords. But this
doesn’t mean you can’t add or target extensions and alterations of
these chords. Utilizing the 9*, 11* and 13% can have a powerful effect.

| won’t go over which extensions or alterations you can use over dif-
ferent qualities of 7* chords in this book. But my other book Zero to
Improv covers this thoroughly.

Let’s finish up with the last motif of this lesson.



Cua? Cun?
Recorded 8va Pay 6 » “ 6 oO. eo . “ 2

For this motif I’ve purposefully tried to use as few notes as possible.
Notice that I’ve even avoided changing any notes in the first 4 bars.
Once again, I’m targeting extensions (the 9") as well.

It’s also a great practice to try playing as few notes as possible over
a given chord progression. Sometimes it can even have a profound ef-
fect to play just one note over an entire sequence. This may or may not
be how you would improvise in a playing situation, but this application
can help you narrow things down in the practice room.

On Green Dolphin Street is a great song to study, and surely there are
more lessons to be learned. But we have three more jazz standard
studies to go, all of which have a lot to teach us.

Let’s move on to the next study: Have You Met Miss Jones.



CHAPTER TEXT>>>
